title: The Almanack of MM — Mental Model Quiz: Part I Wealth A
source: The Almanack of MM by Eric Jorgenson (2020)

questions.question: MM distinguishes between wealth, money, and status. Why does he say 'seek wealth, not money or status,' and what is the structural difference between wealth and money?
questions.short_answer: Wealth is assets that earn while you sleep — factories, code, businesses, investments — whereas money is merely a transferable IOU representing claims on other people's time and labor.
questions.elaborated_answer: Money is a medium of exchange and a social ledger: when you create value, society issues you credits (money) redeemable for future goods or services. It is how wealth moves between people, not wealth itself. Wealth, by contrast, is the underlying productive asset base — businesses, software, capital — that generates output without your continuous presence. This distinction matters because chasing money through labor means you stop earning the moment you stop working. Chasing wealth means building systems that compound independently of your time. Status, meanwhile, is a zero-sum hierarchy game: one person's gain is another's loss, which is why status players frequently attack wealth creators rather than create value themselves.
questions.section: Building Wealth — Understand How Wealth Is Created

questions.question: MM says 'you will get rich by giving society what it wants but does not yet know how to get — at scale.' What does he mean by 'technology,' and why is scale so critical to this formula?
questions.short_answer: Technology, in MM's framing, is anything that doesn't fully work yet — once it works reliably, it stops being technology and becomes a commodity. Scale is what converts a one-off solution into wealth because a single instance serves only one person, while a replicated solution serves millions.
questions.elaborated_answer: MM quotes Alan Kay (attributing it to Danny Hillis): technology is 'the set of things that don't quite work yet.' Once something functions reliably at low cost, it becomes infrastructure. Wealth is captured in the transition period — the moment when you understand how to deliver something people want before society has figured out how to produce it cheaply. Rockefeller captured oil refining; Ford captured mass-production of cars; Jobs captured the smartphone. The scale requirement is structural: if you only build one, you've solved a personal problem. If you build millions, you've captured a market. Code and media have near-zero marginal cost of replication, making them ideal vehicles for achieving scale without proportional increases in effort or capital.
questions.section: Building Wealth — Understand How Wealth Is Created

questions.question: MM claims that 'productize yourself' is a complete summary of his wealth philosophy. What does each half of that phrase map to in terms of his broader framework?
questions.short_answer: 'Yourself' encodes uniqueness, accountability, and specific knowledge — the irreplaceable input. 'Productize' encodes leverage and scalability — turning that unique input into something replicable at scale.
questions.elaborated_answer: The compression is deliberate. 'Yourself' points to specific knowledge (what only you can do authentically), accountability (operating under your own name with skin in the game), and genuine identity (not copying others). 'Productize' points to leverage — choosing a medium (code, capital, media, labor) that scales what you produce beyond your direct time input. The mnemonic also encodes a diagnostic question: 'Is this authentic to me?' (yourself) and 'Am I scaling it, and by what lever?' (productize). MM emphasizes it takes the better part of a decade just to identify what you can uniquely provide, which is why the framework looks simple but is executed slowly.
questions.section: Building Wealth — Understand How Wealth Is Created

questions.question: MM argues there is no skill called 'business' and that you should avoid business magazines and classes. What should you study instead, and what is his underlying reasoning?
questions.short_answer: Study foundational disciplines — microeconomics, game theory, psychology, persuasion, ethics, mathematics, and computers — because these are the timeless mental models that generate leverage, whereas 'business' is a derivative, context-specific application of these deeper fields.
questions.elaborated_answer: MM's reasoning is that 'business' as taught is a collection of case studies, heuristics, and tactics that age quickly and don't transfer across domains. The underlying subjects — game theory, microeconomics, human psychology — are the generative engines from which business insight emerges. Mastering foundations means you can reason from first principles in any novel situation rather than pattern-match to stale examples. He similarly values basic arithmetic and plain English over advanced calculus and elaborate vocabulary, because the former are usable everywhere while the latter are narrowly applicable. The meta-point is that leverage amplifies judgment, so investing in superior judgment (through foundational knowledge) has higher compounding returns than investing in superficial business tactics.
questions.section: Building Wealth — Understand How Wealth Is Created

questions.question: Why does MM say 'specific knowledge cannot be taught, but it can be learned'? What is the structural reason training-based knowledge fails to create competitive advantage?
questions.short_answer: If a skill can be codified into a curriculum and taught at scale, society can train anyone to do it — making it interchangeable and therefore low-margin. Specific knowledge emerges from the intersection of your unique DNA, upbringing, and personal response to experience, which cannot be replicated by institutional instruction.
questions.elaborated_answer: MM's logic is competitive: the moment a skill becomes teachable in a classroom, it becomes a commodity. Anyone with the same curriculum and effort level can acquire it, which means employers can substitute one practitioner for another, driving wages toward the cost of training. Specific knowledge, by contrast, is discovered through apprenticeship, self-directed obsession, and lived experience — not transmitted through formal education. It feels like play to the person who has it, which is the diagnostic signal: if what you're doing feels like effortless absorption compared to how hard it appears to outsiders, you've found a vein of specific knowledge. Because it's rooted in your particular personality and history, no one can be trained to replace you on that exact combination.
questions.section: Building Wealth — Find and Build Specific Knowledge

questions.question: MM's counterintuitive claim is 'escape competition through authenticity.' Why does competition itself indicate you are doing something wrong?
questions.short_answer: You compete with others when you are copying them — pursuing the same goals through the same methods. Authentic expression of your specific knowledge means operating in a category of one, where direct competition is structurally impossible.
questions.elaborated_answer: Competition is a signal of sameness: when many people pursue identical credentials, markets, or approaches, they enter a zero-sum fight that erodes margins and morale for everyone. MM's insight is that this is avoidable by design. Joe Rogan, Scott Adams, and Bill Watterson don't have direct competitors because what they produce is a genuine extension of who they are — their specific knowledge deployed authentically through a scalable medium. Someone could attempt to copy the format, but not the underlying person. The internet makes this strategy more viable than ever: it allows any niche authentic voice to find a global audience large enough to build a business. The corollary is that if you feel intensely competitive pressure, it's diagnostic — you've probably drifted toward copying rather than authentic differentiation.
questions.section: Building Wealth — Find and Build Specific Knowledge

questions.question: MM says the most important skill for getting rich today is becoming a 'perpetual learner' who can come up to speed on a new field in nine to twelve months. Why does this replace the traditional model, and what makes foundations so critical to this?
questions.short_answer: Fields now emerge and become obsolete on a five-year cycle, making any fixed expertise increasingly irrelevant. The ability to rapidly acquire competence in new domains — built on strong foundational reasoning skills — is therefore more durable and valuable than any specific credential.
questions.elaborated_answer: The old model assumed fields were stable: study a profession for four years, practice it for thirty. That model breaks when the half-life of domain knowledge contracts to a few years. The competitive advantage shifts to the person who can learn fastest, not the person with the deepest static expertise in a legacy field. Foundations — basic numeracy, clear reasoning, persuasive communication — are what make rapid learning possible, because they are the scaffolding onto which new knowledge attaches. MM distinguishes being 9/10 on foundations from being 10/10 on a narrow specialty: foundations transfer across every new field, while deep specialty knowledge in a dying domain is a sunk cost. This is also why he values reading — it's faster than listening, and books are dense with foundational models that outlast any particular application.
questions.section: Building Wealth — Find and Build Specific Knowledge

questions.question: MM says specific knowledge is 'often at the edge of knowledge' and that if you're not 100 percent into it, someone who is will outperform you 'by a lot, not a little.' Why does the domain of ideas produce such extreme outperformance gaps?
questions.short_answer: In domains of ideas and knowledge work, compound interest and leverage both apply simultaneously — someone with slightly greater passion and insight produces exponentially better output over time, not just marginally better output.
questions.elaborated_answer: Physical labor has diminishing returns: a person twice as motivated may work twice as many hours, producing roughly twice the output. But in knowledge work at the frontier, the gap compounds. The person who is 100 percent into a subject reads more, makes more connections, generates better hypotheses, and iterates faster — and each cycle builds on the previous one. Because these domains also involve leverage (code, media, networks), the output of the obsessed person gets multiplied across millions of users or readers, while the moderately interested person's output reaches far fewer. MM's point is not that you should work harder but that you should find the intersection where your obsession is natural, because that person will always beat someone performing interest. Passion is not a luxury — at the frontier of ideas, it's a structural competitive advantage.
questions.section: Building Wealth — Find and Build Specific Knowledge

questions.principle: Play Long-Term Games with Long-Term People
questions.question: MM extends compound interest beyond finance to reputation and relationships. What is the mechanism by which reputation compounds, and why is it potentially more valuable than direct skill acquisition?
questions.short_answer: Reputation compounds because each trustworthy action makes future high-stakes opportunities more accessible — people route deals, capital, and partnerships to those with established integrity, creating an accelerating return that cannot be faked or fast-tracked.
questions.elaborated_answer: MM observes that the top positions in society — CEOs, fund managers, senior partners — are typically held by people whose relationships and track records have compounded over decades, not by whoever was objectively most talented at age thirty. The mechanism is trust: each visible instance of high-integrity behavior deposits into a reputational ledger that others observe and remember. Over years, this ledger becomes a powerful filter that routes disproportionate opportunity toward you. MM's example with Elad Gil illustrates the flywheel: because Elad reliably over-delivers, MM routes him every deal, which in turn compounds Elad's network and reputation further. The counterintuitive implication is that being strategically generous and visibly honest in small transactions has outsized long-run returns — far exceeding what optimizing individual deals would produce.
questions.section: Building Wealth — Play Long-Term Games with Long-Term People

questions.principle: Play Long-Term Games with Long-Term People
questions.question: MM says '99 percent of effort is wasted' and yet does not say you should skip the 99 percent. What is his actual prescription, and why is the 99 percent necessary at all?
questions.short_answer: The 99 percent is necessary because you cannot identify the 1 percent without exploring broadly — but once you recognize the rare thing you can go all-in on for life, you should exit everything else immediately and invest fully in that single compound-interest path.
questions.elaborated_answer: MM's argument is not nihilistic about effort but diagnostic about when to stop. Most exploration — dating partners you won't marry, learning subjects you won't use, working jobs that don't compound — is necessary search behavior because the 1 percent is not obvious in advance. The error is not doing the exploration; it is continuing the exploration after you've found the signal. The moment you recognize a relationship, field, or partner that can compound for decades, staying in mediocre alternatives is the real waste. The insight is about decision speed: most people keep running the 99 percent long after they've found the 1 percent, because inertia and social pressure override the rational exit. MM's prescription is to treat that recognition as an unconditional trigger to reallocate entirely.
questions.section: Building Wealth — Play Long-Term Games with Long-Term People

questions.principle: Play Long-Term Games with Long-Term People
questions.question: MM states 'intentions don't matter, actions do — that's why being ethical is hard.' What does this reveal about how he thinks reputation and trust are actually built?
questions.short_answer: Trust is built from observable, repeated behavior — not from stated values or good intentions. This makes ethics genuinely difficult because it requires sustained costly action, not just correct belief.
questions.elaborated_answer: This is one of MM's sharpest asymmetries: the world evaluates you on what you actually do under pressure, not on what you meant or wanted to do. Intentions are private and unverifiable; actions are public and compound in both directions. An ethical reputation requires that you take the costly action even when no one is watching and even when it's personally disadvantageous — because the pattern of behavior, observed over time, is what others use to predict your future conduct. This is why 'being ethical is hard': it's not primarily an intellectual challenge (knowing the right thing) but a behavioral one (consistently doing the costly right thing). For long-term games, this means the investment in ethical action early — when the personal cost is highest relative to the reputational payoff — is exactly when it matters most.
questions.section: Building Wealth — Play Long-Term Games with Long-Term People

questions.question: MM says accountability is a 'double-edged sword' that is both a prerequisite for leverage and a social risk most people avoid. What specific forms of leverage require public accountability, and why can't they be obtained anonymously?
questions.short_answer: Capital and labor — the two permissioned forms of leverage — require others to voluntarily grant them to you, which only happens when they can assess your track record. That assessment is only possible if you operate publicly under your own name.
questions.elaborated_answer: MM distinguishes permissioned leverage (capital and labor) from permissionless leverage (code and media). To get people to give you money to manage or to follow your leadership, they must first trust you — and trust requires a visible, verifiable history of judgment and integrity. Operating anonymously removes the reputational signal that makes this trust formation possible. Taking risks under your own name creates a credibility ledger: successful bets and honest failures are both attributed to you, building the pattern recognition that others need before they'll hand you capital or loyalty. MM's own example — publicly posting about philosophy and crypto before they were mainstream — illustrates this: the short-term social risk of looking unconventional was the price for building the authentic reputation that attracted the right audience and opportunities long-term.

questions.question: MM argues that 'the downside risk is not that large' in modern society, and that people therefore take on far less accountability than they rationally should. What psychological mechanism does he think overrides this rational calculation?
questions.short_answer: Humans are socially hardwired to fear public failure under their own names — a visceral aversion rooted in evolutionary tribal logic that no longer maps onto the actual low-stakes outcomes of modern business failure.
questions.elaborated_answer: MM points out the mismatch directly: modern society has no debtors' prisons, and even personal bankruptcy can clear debts in well-functioning ecosystems. Communities like Silicon Valley specifically normalize and forgive high-integrity failures. Yet the social wiring to avoid public failure evolved in small tribal contexts where status loss had severe survival consequences — ostracism could be fatal. That ancient circuitry fires the same alarm signal when facing modern reputational risk, even though the actual consequences are mild and recoverable. The result is systematic under-accountability: people hide behind corporate structures, anonymous handles, or cautious hedging, forgoing the credibility-building upside because the emotional cost of potential public failure feels disproportionately large. MM's prescription is to consciously override this wiring and recognize that the people who tolerate public risk are the ones who accumulate the credibility and leverage that others cannot.

questions.question: MM says 'embrace accountability and take business risks under your own name — society will reward you with responsibility, equity, and leverage.' Why does society specifically reward accountability with these three things rather than just money?
questions.short_answer: Society needs trusted agents to manage resources, lead people, and take on productive risk — accountability signals that you are willing to bear costs when things go wrong, making you a credible candidate for these roles and the equity that comes with them.
questions.elaborated_answer: Responsibility, equity, and leverage are not simply purchased with effort — they are granted by others who need to trust the person receiving them. Society's core coordination problem is finding agents who won't defect when given control over capital, people, or decisions. Public accountability under your own name is the most reliable credibility signal available, because it means the costs of failure fall on you personally. This creates alignment: an accountable person has skin in the game that matches the interests of those delegating responsibility. Equity specifically flows to the person bearing risk, not just doing work — which is why founders and investors get equity while employees get wages. Leverage follows from the same logic: no one gives you a multiplier on their resources unless they trust your judgment won't destroy them.

questions.principle: Build or Buy Equity in a Business
questions.question: MM claims that renting out your time — even at very high rates like a lawyer or doctor — structurally cannot produce real wealth. What is the specific linear constraint that makes this true regardless of hourly rate?
questions.short_answer: When income is tied to hours worked, output is capped by the finite hours in a day and stops entirely when you stop working — making it mathematically impossible to earn nonlinearly or to generate income while sleeping, retired, or on vacation.
questions.elaborated_answer: The problem is not the size of the hourly rate but the linearity of the relationship between input and output. Even a surgeon billing $5,000 an hour earns zero the moment they stop operating. There is no leverage in the equation — no mechanism by which a past unit of work continues generating value beyond its immediate billing. Wealth, by contrast, is defined by assets that earn while you sleep: a business with a brand, a software product, a portfolio of investments. These continue generating returns without your direct participation. MM notes that even doctors who become genuinely wealthy typically did so by building a private practice with a brand, developing proprietary medical IP, or otherwise transitioning from labor-as-input to equity-as-asset. The wage structure, however high, keeps them trapped in a linear model where retirement means income stops.
questions.section: Building Wealth — Build or Buy Equity in a Business

questions.principle: Build or Buy Equity in a Business
questions.question: MM contrasts owning equity with owning debt, arguing you always want to own equity. What does this reveal about his broader theory of wealth creation versus wealth preservation?
questions.short_answer: Debt ownership provides guaranteed but capped returns — you own the downside protection but forfeit participation in upside. Equity ownership means you own the upside of a productive enterprise, which is where wealth is actually created rather than merely preserved.
questions.elaborated_answer: Debt instruments (bonds, loans, fixed income) are designed for capital preservation: you get your money back plus a predictable return, but your gain is contractually limited. Equity is structurally different — you own a proportional claim on the future value created by the enterprise, with no ceiling on the upside. MM's point is that real wealth is built in the upside, not protected in the downside. Someone who is perpetually optimizing for capital safety — keeping money in bonds, working stable salaried jobs — is optimizing for the wrong variable. Wealth accumulation requires accepting asymmetric risk: you can lose your equity stake entirely, but the expected value of owning productive businesses over time vastly exceeds the expected value of owning claims on fixed returns. This is why every person MM identifies as having 'really made money' owns some form of product, business, or intellectual property — not just a wage or a bond portfolio.
questions.section: Building Wealth — Build or Buy Equity in a Business

questions.principle: Build or Buy Equity in a Business
questions.question: MM says your employer will pay you 'the bare minimum they have to' to keep you doing their job, no matter how high that minimum is. What structural dynamic explains this, and what is the only escape?
questions.short_answer: When you work for someone else, you are exchanging your labor for a wage while they capture the accountability, brand, intellectual property, and equity upside — the parts of the value chain that generate compounding returns. The only escape is to own those elements yourself.
questions.elaborated_answer: An employer's incentive is to capture the spread between the value you create and what they pay you — that spread is their profit. Competition for talent raises wages, but it doesn't change the fundamental structure: the employee still bears no ownership of the brand, IP, or equity that compounds over time. The employer took on the risk, built the brand, and structured the accountability — so the employer captures the nonlinear upside. MM's argument is not that employees are exploited in a moral sense, but that the wage structure is inherently linear and bounded: it can only ever be 'the bare minimum to keep you.' To earn beyond that — to participate in the compounding upside — requires owning a piece of what you're building, whether through founding, co-founding, stock options, or investment. The transition from wage worker to equity owner is the structural move that makes financial freedom possible.
questions.section: Building Wealth — Build or Buy Equity in a Business
